questions.question: A patient has a heart rate of 45/min with normal blood pressure, clear mentation, and no chest pain. What is the correct initial action?
options: A. Give atropine 1 mg IV immediately
B. Begin transcutaneous pacing
C. Monitor and observe while investigating the cause
D. Start a dopamine infusion
questions.rationale: Asymptomatic bradycardia without signs of instability is observed and investigated, not immediately treated with drugs or pacing.

questions.question: What is the correct first-line drug dose for symptomatic bradycardia?
options: A. Atropine 0.5 mg IV once
B. Atropine 1 mg IV/IO, may repeat q3-5min to max 3 mg
C. Epinephrine 1 mg IV every 3-5 min
D. Atropine 3 mg IV as a single dose
questions.rationale: Atropine 1 mg IV or IO is given first, repeated every 3–5 minutes to a maximum total of 3 mg.

questions.question: A patient with symptomatic bradycardia has a wide-QRS third-degree AV block. Atropine 1 mg has just been given. What is the best next step?
options: A. Repeat atropine and wait to see if it works
B. Prepare for transcutaneous pacing and/or an infusion without delay, since infranodal block is unlikely to respond to atropine
C. Give a second 3 mg dose of atropine immediately
D. Discharge the patient
questions.rationale: High-grade/third-degree block with a wide QRS is infranodal and unlikely to respond to atropine; pacing or an infusion should not be delayed by repeated ineffective atropine dosing.

questions.question: What is the maximum total dose of atropine for bradycardia?
options: A. 1 mg
B. 2 mg
C. 3 mg
D. 5 mg
questions.rationale: Atropine is dosed 1 mg IV/IO, repeated every 3–5 minutes, to a maximum total of 3 mg.

questions.question: Transcutaneous pacing has been started. Pacing spikes with a wide QRS are seen on the monitor, but no pulse is palpable at the paced rate. What is the correct interpretation?
options: A. This is adequate capture; continue as is
B. Electrical capture without mechanical capture — treat as if pacing is not working (consider PEA/shock)
C. Increase the pacing rate only
D. This confirms successful pacing
questions.rationale: Electrical capture (spike + QRS) without a corresponding pulse means there is no effective mechanical capture; the patient's perfusion status must be reassessed.

questions.question: Which two second-line options can be used when atropine fails in symptomatic bradycardia?
options: A. Amiodarone and lidocaine
B. Transcutaneous pacing and dopamine or epinephrine infusion
C. Adenosine and vagal maneuvers
D. Synchronized cardioversion and magnesium
questions.rationale: When atropine is ineffective, transcutaneous pacing and dopamine or epinephrine infusion are the recommended next-line therapies.

questions.question: A conscious patient is undergoing transcutaneous pacing. What should be provided if hemodynamically tolerated?
options: A. General anesthesia
B. Analgesia/sedation for the discomfort of pacing
C. Nothing is needed
D. Neuromuscular blockade only
questions.rationale: Transcutaneous pacing is uncomfortable due to skeletal muscle contraction; analgesia/sedation should be given if the patient can tolerate it hemodynamically.

questions.question: Which of the following is a reversible cause of bradycardia that should be actively sought?
options: A. Beta-blocker toxicity
B. Recent tetanus vaccination
C. Seasonal allergies
D. Mild dehydration only
questions.rationale: Beta-blocker, calcium-channel blocker, and digoxin toxicity, hyperkalemia, hypothyroidism, inferior MI, and increased ICP are all reversible/underlying causes to investigate.

questions.question: Why is atropine more effective for sinus node dysfunction than for a Mobitz type II block?
options: A. Atropine works by blocking vagal tone at the SA/AV node, and Mobitz II is an infranodal block not mediated by vagal tone
B. Atropine has no mechanism relevant to bradycardia
C. Mobitz II always resolves spontaneously
D. Atropine only works on wide-QRS rhythms
questions.rationale: Atropine's antivagal mechanism is effective at the SA/AV node level but does not address infranodal conduction disease, which is why escalation is needed for Mobitz II/third-degree block.

questions.question: A patient becomes hypotensive and confused with a heart rate of 38/min. What is this classified as?
options: A. Asymptomatic bradycardia
B. Symptomatic (unstable) bradycardia
C. Normal sinus rhythm
D. Tachycardia
questions.rationale: Hypotension and altered mental status with a slow rate meet the definition of symptomatic/unstable bradycardia requiring treatment.

questions.question: What starting pacing rate is commonly used for transcutaneous pacing?
options: D. 250/min
questions.rationale: A common starting target for transcutaneous pacing is 60–80/min, titrated to clinical response.

questions.question: Which statement about dopamine and epinephrine infusions in bradycardia is correct?
options: A. They must always follow pacing and cannot be started first
B. They are alternative second-line options that may be used instead of or alongside pacing
C. They are first-line before atropine
D. They are contraindicated in bradycardia
questions.rationale: Dopamine and epinephrine infusions are alternative second-line options, useful especially when pacing is unavailable or not tolerated.

questions.question: A bradycardic patient is found to have severe hyperkalemia. What is an important part of management?
options: A. Ignore the potassium and only give atropine
B. Treat the hyperkalemia as part of addressing the underlying/reversible cause of bradycardia
C. Withhold all bradycardia treatment until dialysis
D. Give atropine 5 mg immediately
questions.rationale: Bradycardia may be a manifestation of a reversible metabolic cause; treating hyperkalemia directly addresses the underlying problem.

questions.question: What confirms successful transcutaneous pacing capture?
options: A. A pacing spike alone on the monitor
B. A paced spike immediately followed by a wide QRS/T wave, with a palpable pulse matching the paced rate
C. Patient verbal report only
D. Any heart rate above 60/min
questions.rationale: Both electrical capture (spike-QRS-T) and mechanical capture (a palpable pulse at the paced rate) must be confirmed.

questions.question: A patient with symptomatic bradycardia due to suspected calcium-channel blocker overdose is not responding to standard atropine dosing. What is a reasonable next consideration?
options: A. Continue atropine indefinitely
B. Escalate to pacing/infusion and treat the toxin-specific cause per toxicology guidance in parallel
C. Give more atropine beyond 3 mg total
D. No further action is needed
questions.rationale: Escalation to pacing or infusion should proceed without delay while the reversible toxic cause is addressed in parallel.

questions.question: Which of these findings would classify bradycardia as symptomatic/unstable?
options: A. Mild fatigue only
B. Acute heart failure signs
C. Normal blood pressure and clear mentation
D. Rate of 55/min with no symptoms
questions.rationale: Acute heart failure is one of the defined signs of cardiopulmonary compromise that makes bradycardia symptomatic and warrants treatment.

questions.question: Why should a provider avoid repeatedly re-dosing atropine beyond the maximum in a deteriorating bradycardic patient?
options: A. Because it has no ceiling effect and is always safe to keep giving
B. Because further doses beyond 3 mg add little benefit and delay effective escalation to pacing/infusion
C. Because atropine is contraindicated in all bradycardia
D. Because it must always be given with epinephrine
questions.rationale: Atropine has a defined maximum total dose of 3 mg; exceeding this without benefit only delays more effective escalation therapy.
